# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create Kubernetes deployment manifests for microservices. Add annotations to control Istio sidecar injection behavior. Set sidecar.istio.io/inject to manage proxy injection per deployment. This is for our service mesh deployment strategy.
# Category: istio_sidecar_injection_disabled

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: microservice-deploimentment # replace with your desired name, e.g., mymicroservicesapp
spec:
  replicas: 3                    # modify this value to set the number of pods that you want in each deployment instance (e.g.: if there are three services)
                                  # If using a kube-proxy sidecar injection for your service mesh, then we should not need these fields because Istio will handle it automatically through its configuration file(configmap). 
                                                          # For manual setup of the Kubernetes deployment with annotations and labels: e.g., use following yaml files as reference to create custom deployments manually or using kubectl command line tool for more specific cases (like setting sidecar injection behavior)  
labels:                         # set these fields based on your needs, you can also leave them empty if not necessary in this case 
    app.kubernetes.io/name: microservice-deployment     # replace with the name of deployment instance e.g., mymicroservicesapp (use lowercase and hyphen separated names)  
    serviceMeshInjectionSidecar_istio.io_inject : "true"  # annotations added to enable sidecar injection, you can customize these fields according your needs or leave them empty if not necessary in this case for the most part (Istio will handle it automatically via its configuration file)
---  
apiVersion: v1                    # version of Kubernetes resource that was created by yaml files.  This should be apps/v1 unless you're using a different API group e.g., networking or autoscaling etc, but in general we use the same versions as our resources for consistency and easier updates
kind: Service                     # type of Kubernetes resource that was created by yaml files   (e.g.: Deployment) 
metadata:                        # metadata associated with a specific object/resource like name or labels which could be set in this part to match your needs, e.g., mymicroservicesapp-service for the service's label value and so on   
      name : microservice         # replace it according you want (e.g.: helloworld)  Also note that names should not contain capital letters or special characters like / , . etc as these will be treated by Kubernetes to create a DNS entry for the service, e.g., mymicroservicesapp-mynamespace
spec:                           # this part describes how your Service/Pods are configured and managed in terms of networking related rules  (e.g.: selectors used , ports exposed etc)  
      type : LoadBalancer        # specifying that the service should use a load balancing solution like kube-proxy or external DNS to expose it, e.g., Ingress controller is set as nginx by default in Kubernetes  (if not specified otherwise). This can be either 'ClusterIP' if you only want one pod per node and have the service itself accessible from any location inside cluster; else use LoadBalancer which will create an external load balancing solution.
      ports:                      # these fields describe how your Service/Pods are configured in terms of networking related rules  (e.g.: selectors used , port exposed etc)  
          - name : http            # replace it according you want e.g., mymicroservicesapp-http for the service's label value and so on    Also note that names should not contain capital letters or special characters like / . as these will be treated by Kubernetes to create a DNS entry  (e.g.: helloworld)
            port: 80              # replace it according you want e.g., mymicroservicesapp-http for the service's label value and so on    Also note that ports should not contain capital letters or special characters like / . as these will be treated by Kubernetes to create a DNS entry (e.g.: helloworld)
            targetPort: 80         # replace it according you want e.g., mymicroservicesapp-http for the service's label value and so on    Also note that ports should not contain capital letters or special characters like / . as these will be treated by Kubernetes to create a DNS entry (e.g.: helloworld)
